serving.natural_language_portion: One typical restaurant serving of Murgh Aloo is approximately 300 g, comprising a generous portion of spiced chicken pieces and baby potatoes in a semi-dry masala sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken curry with potato — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 revised edition), composite dish estimate
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Murgh Aloo is a North Indian chicken and potato curry. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) composite entries for chicken curry (cooked, with gravy) and boiled potato, weighted approximately 65% chicken curry component and 35% potato component by edible weight, reflecting a typical restaurant preparation. Energy and macros cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for chicken curry (NDB 22904) and boiled potato (NDB 11365).

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on chicken curry with potato
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement found for Murgh Aloo. GI estimated at 52 based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for mixed chicken and potato curry (GI ~50–54, low category). Potato component (boiled, GI ~50 per Sydney GI Database) dominates the carbohydrate fraction; spices and protein from chicken lower the effective GI. Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (52 × 8.4 × 3.0) / 100 = 13.1 g. GI category: low (≤55).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~320 mg per 100 g; ~960 mg per restaurant serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150–200 g and request reduced salt preparation, as a full 300 g restaurant serving contributes approximately 960 mg sodium, representing 40–48% of the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from potato carbohydrates (GL ~13 per serving) combined with adequate protein
type2_diabetes.guidance: Murgh Aloo has a low GI (~52) due to the protein and fat content moderating glucose absorption, but the glycemic load per 300 g serving is approximately 13 g; people with Type 2 Diabetes should consume a controlled portion of 150–200 g alongside non-starchy vegetables to manage postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~2.1 g per 100 g) and sodium (~320 mg per 100 g) from cooking oil and spice base
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The saturated fat content of approximately 6.3 g per 300 g serving is within acceptable limits for most cardiovascular patients, but the sodium load (~960 mg per serving) warrants caution; choosing lean chicken breast, minimal added oil, and requesting low-salt preparation can reduce cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Murgh Aloo (Hindi: मुर्ग आलू) is a classic North Indian chicken and potato curry in which marinated chicken pieces — often prepared tikka-style — are stir-fried or sautéed together with baby potatoes, green chillies, onion, tomato, garlic, ginger, and a blend of home-pounded whole spices such as cumin, coriander, garam masala, and turmeric. The dish is semi-dry to lightly sauced, with a rich, aromatic masala coating the chicken and potatoes. The texture contrasts tender, spiced chicken with soft, flavour-absorbing baby potatoes. Murgh Aloo is a staple of North Indian home cooking and restaurant menus across India and the Indian diaspora in Singapore, the UK, and the Gulf. Nutritionally, it provides a moderate-protein, moderate-fat profile (~12.5 g protein and ~7.8 g fat per 100 g) with a meaningful carbohydrate contribution from the potato (~8.4 g per 100 g). The glycemic index is estimated at 52 (low), making it more suitable for blood glucose management than pure potato dishes. Sodium content is moderate to high in restaurant preparations (~320 mg per 100 g), requiring portion awareness for hypertensive individuals. The dish is naturally gluten-free and typically halal. It is commonly served with naan, roti, or steamed basmati rice as part of a larger Indian meal.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Murgh Aloo?
Is Murgh Aloo good for diabetics?
What is the difference between Murgh Aloo and Aloo Gosht?

meal_context: part of a multi-dish Indian spread

confidence_reason: Murgh Aloo is a well-documented North Indian dish. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN composite entries for chicken curry and potato, weighted by typical recipe proportions, and cross-checked against USDA SR Legacy. No exact ICMR-NIN entry for Murgh Aloo as a named dish exists; hence confidence is medium rather than high. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) composite entries for chicken curry and boiled potato (~65:35 weight ratio), cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. No single exact entry for Murgh Aloo exists in any approved primary source; source_match_type is 'adapted'. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this dish category. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this dish category. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this dish category. 3. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for mixed chicken and potato curry; gi_category is low (GI ≤ 55). 4. The input food_group 'Vegetables' has been corrected to 'Meat and alternatives' as the primary ingredient is chicken. 5. Halal status is assumed based on the restaurant context (Kinara – Boat Quay, an Indian restaurant in Singapore); not independently verified. 6. trans_fat_g of 0.1 g per 100 g reflects trace amounts from cooking oil; this is a plausible low value, not confirmed zero.
